data.title: Oshkosh: A Company With Long-Term Reliability But With Short-Term Headwinds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My DCF analysis suggests that Oshkosh Corporation (NYSE:OSK) is overvalued by 27%. However, its valuation metrics show that its potential growth is not fully priced in. The company has strong long-term contracts and follows an M&A growth strategy. There are some temporary headwinds for the company, such as the end of the Caterpillar-branded telehandlers contract, which directly affected consolidated sales. I give the stock a Hold rating.

Company Overview

Oshkosh Corporation, which manufactures and distributes special vehicles for different industries worldwide, operates through the following segments: Access, Transport, and Vocational. The Access segment is responsible for manufacturing aerial work and telehandlers for industrial purposes. The Transport segment manufactures tactical vehicles and related services for defense. The Vocational segment provides firefighting equipment and emergency response vehicles. The company was founded in 1917 and is based in Oshkosh, Wisconsin.

As shown in the table below, the company has done multiple acquisitions in recent years, with Hinowa, Oshkosh AeroTech (the renamed entity after the acquisition of the AeroTech business from JBT), and AUSA being the most notable. In March 2023, Oshkosh closed the acquisition of Hinowa. This acquisition offers Oshkosh the opportunity to expand globally in the Access segment. The main reasons for the acquisition, in my view, are the 250,000-square-foot manufacturing facility located in Nogara, Italy, and the opportunity to own the producer of JLG crawler boom lifts.

In August 2023, Oshkosh closed the transaction with JBT that led to the company's AeroTech business. This acquisition is key, as Aerotech's own brands serve around 75% of air travelers at U.S. airports and load around 79% of overnight express packages globally. In September 2024, Oshkosh closed the acquisition of AUSA. AUSA products will complement JLG's, Oshkosh's subsidiary, line of telehandlers. AUSA has worked with JLG in the past, manufacturing the SkyTrak 3013 compact telehander for JLG. These acquisitions are driving Oshkosh's expansion, with AUSA and Hinowa supporting growth in the Access segment and lowering the negative effect on consolidated sales. Oshkosh AeroTech contributed to the segment backlog and consolidated sales performance.

Segment Overview

Access

Sales for the Access segment decreased by 10.7% in Q2 because Oshkosh couldn't produce Caterpillar-branded telehandlers because the agreement between the two firms had expired. However, AUSA's volume sales partially offset this negative effect. This was reflected in the decrease in order backlog of 63.6%, from $3,264.4 million in 2024 to $1,189.0 million in 2025.

Additionally, tariffs may further impact Oshkosh's Access segment, as 407 product categories are cataloged as "derivative" with a 50% tariff on aluminum and steel products. However, the company's execution efforts, such as M&A, are expected to reduce the tariff impact compared to Q1. This segment may be impacted by the previously mentioned factors in Q3 and in the short term. However, its recent acquisitions reinforce its operational focus on long-term growth.

Transport

Segment sales decreased 16.2% in Q2 due to the end of the Joint Light Tactical Vehicle program, though the sales were partly offset by the Next Generation Delivery Vehicle (NGDV) production for the U.S. Postal Service, or USPS. However, the backlog for the segment showed an increase of 5.1% YoY, which means that even after a high-relevance contract has ended, the company has managed to compensate for that by entering into other long-term contracts. The USPS contract is for replacing the "old" Legacy Delivery Mail Vehicle (LLV) with the NGDV. Oshkosh has 51,500 NGDVs on contract that has a potential lifespan of 20 years. With this contract, the company has the opportunity to replace the approximately 130,000 LLVs, with the youngest being 31 years old, with around 165,000 NGDVs.

The macro context for this segment also looks solid in the long term, as it is supported by the federal government's current expenditure. In my view, with these levels of government expenditures, the transport segment can replace all the "old" LLVs.

Vocational

Sales for this segment increased by 15% in Q2 due to volume increases and enhanced pricing. The backlog showed strong growth as it increased 10.4% YoY, from $5,678.2 million to $6,268 million. Oshkosh AeroTech also contributed to this strong performance, as it allowed the company the opportunity to operate in the airport market. With AeroTech, Oshkosh is leveraging its global growth potential in Europe, the Middle East, and Asia. Also, this acquisition has enabled product innovation, such as for the Autonomous Cargo Handler, shared in the investor day presentation.

Takeaway

Despite the expiration of key contracts, the company shows long-term reliability. The Access segment suffered from the expiration of the Caterpillar contract, but recent acquisitions helped to reduce the negative impact partially. The Transport segment saw a decline in sales due to the end of the JLTV program, but this was pegged by the increase in order backlog because of the USPS NGDV contract. The Vocational segment presented positive sales and a backlog, reflecting growth for Oshkosh Aerotech. With its recent acquisitions and new contracts, I believe Oshkosh is in the right position to recover from this drawback.

Quarterly Financial Results

Oshkosh released its Q2 2025 financial results on August 1, delivering strong results overall but with some headwinds.

On the positive side, we have the following:

  • Net income accounted for $204.8 million, a 21.5% YoY increase.
  • EPS increased from $2.56 to $3.16, or up 23.4% YoY.
  • Operating income increased by 11.8%.
  • Corporate and other costs accounted for $55 million, a 47% YOY decrease.

On the negative side:

  • Consolidated sales decreased by 4% year over year
  • The tax rate for pre-tax income accounted for 24.1%, a 0.7% YOY increase

These financial results indicate that profitability is still growing even though sales have decreased. The main reason why consolidated sales fell in the quarter was the bad performance in the Access and Transport segments. Profitability increased due to the 47.12% YoY decrease in corporate and other expenses and the good sales performance in the Vocational segment.

Oshkosh's overall strategy is to grow through mergers and acquisitions. This is reflected in the 10.4% YoY increase in consolidated sales for the Vocational segment, with AeroTech being a key growth contributor.

My outlook for Q3 is bullish despite the sales decline in the Access and Transport segments. The AUSA and Hinowa acquisitions are reducing the negative effects of the Caterpillar contract expiration, and the NGDV USPS contract is driving the order backlog in the Transport business to the positive range. As noted above, government expenditure trends also support the opportunity arising from the USPS contract to replace "old" delivery vehicles. Moreover, long-term contracts offer the company a competitive advantage compared to peers such as Terex (TEX) that lack similar contracts.

Additionally, the Vocational segment has shown strong backlog and sales due to the acquisition of Oshkosh Aerotech, which opened the opportunity to operate in a new market.

Short-Term Headwinds

The main headwind is the expiration of the Caterpillar Telehandler contract, which led to the 63.3% decrease in order backlog in the Access segment. Further, the end of the Joint Light Tactical Vehicle program in the Transport segment led to a 16.2% decline in segment sales. I, however, view these as temporary issues, and I believe the company is in the right position to recover. The recent acquisitions, such as AUSA and Hinowa, are helping to offset the negative effect of the Caterpillar contract expiry, while the USPS contract has the opportunity to replace all the "old" delivery vehicles and has a potential 20-year lifespan.

Tariffs are another short-term headwind for Oshkosh, as they can directly impact its cost of production. However, I believe tariffs will not affect performance compared to Q1 and its acquisitions, plus the USPS contract, are driving performance, as reflected in the increase in order backlogs for the Transport and Vocational segments.

Valuation: DCF Analysis

A DCF analysis shows that Oshkosh is significantly overvalued. I calculated WACC assuming a cost of debt of 7%, a cost of equity of 9.31%, and a capital structure that has 12.16% debt and 87.84% equity. The pre-tax cost of debt was calculated by dividing the interest expense by the total debt. The cost of debt after tax was calculated using this formula: Cost of Debt*(1-Effective Tax Rate). In my model, I expect that the company keeps its current debt levels, or in a similar low-level range.

I calculated the cost of equity using the following formula: (Risk Free Rate)+Beta*(Market Return-Risk Free Rate). The risk-free rate is the yield of the 10Y U.S. Treasury Bonds, and the market return is an estimated average of the S&P500 Index (SP500) over the years. The capital structure breakdown uses the company's market capitalization as equity and total debt as debt, as depicted in the WACC table below.

Moreover, the growth rate used to calculate the FFCF was the rate the construction machinery global market is expected to grow annually. This rate avoids historical volatility and mirrors sector trends. The perpetual growth rate is set to replicate the long-term GDP performance of the U.S. economy to project that the company will keep growing at a moderate rate.

The formula I used to calculate equity value was adding the sum of FCF and the cash on hand and then subtracting it from the total debt. Finally, the DCF price per share was calculated by just dividing the equity value by the shares outstanding.

The fair value derived by my DCF analysis is approximately $109; compared to the current price, this represents a potential overvaluation of around 18.21%.

2028 Financial Targets: Are the numbers achievable?

In June 2025, the company announced its 2028 financial targets. In my view, the revenue target of $13-$14 billion (which is 30.48% above current levels) seems achievable, as the current annual growth rate of 7-8%, which is similar to the CAGR for the construction machinery market, would take the company closer to that figure. This rate mirrors sector trends and provides a conservative estimate of the company's growth. I believe the adjusted EPS target is also achievable, considering this growth estimate.

Competitive Scene

Terex Corporation has the closest brand when compared to Oshkosh's Access segment. As shown below, both companies have delivered a similar EBITDA margin over the years. Oshkosh's competitive advantage is its long-term contracts, such as the NGDV program, which makes the company strongly reliable in the long term and highly profitable as well (as was shown in its Q2 performance).

Terex doesn't have a segment or any products similar to Oshkosh's Transport and Vocational segments. This indicates that Oshkosh has more diversified revenue streams with strategic positioning and the ability to win key contracts.

According to a Business Research Company study, the global construction machinery market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 8.1% from 2024 to 2029. Oshkosh maintains its profitability due to its long-term contracts and its M&A strategy. I estimate that EBITDA margin will remain above 10% in the long term if the company continues to deliver on profitability.

As can be seen from the table below, Oshkosh ranks high vs. peers on key metrics even as its valuation is low compared to the sector median. This shows that Oshkosh's future potential growth isn't priced in.

The table below represents the profitability metrics for Oshkosh and Terex. Oshkosh presents more steady profitability metrics than Terex. Both companies have similar gross profit margins, but Oshkosh's net income margin is twice as big as Terex. This means that even as both companies have a similar level of production efficiency, Oshkosh can generate double the profitability of its competitor.

Risks

The company is highly sensitive to industry and economic trends. In the event of a downturn, Oshkosh would be affected negatively, which can reflect on its share price too.

Another area of concern is its dependency on contracts. For example, the end of the Caterpillar-branded telehander contract directly affected sales in the Access segment. Additionally, the company depends on government programs such as the Joint Light Tactical Vehicle and the Next Generation Vehicle production program. This dependency was reflected in Oshkosh's consolidated sales. These risks may negatively impact Oshkosh in Q3, but still, my expectations for the next quarter are solid.

The third area of concern is tariffs on steel products, as they may impact the production cost of Oshkosh's products. However, the company expects a lower impact in 2025 compared to the previous quarter.

Conclusion

In conclusion, my DCF-derived price per share suggests the OSK stock is significantly overvalued, but it offers long-term reliability. The company suffers from different short-term headwinds, such as the decline in the Access segment backlog, the expiration of key contracts, and tariffs that may impact the cost of its products. However, contracts such as the USPS NGDV contract and recent acquisitions highlight long-term potential, and so I assign a Hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Simon Property Group Is Deftly Handling Changing Tastes
data.description:
Simon Property Group, Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:SPG) has underperformed the market YTD, including with high single-digit underperformance since we last recommended against investing. Despite that, the company is intelligently tackling a new wave of modern malls, with an impressive portfolio of assets, and the ability to continue driving strong returns.

Simon Property Group Q2 2025 Results

Simon Property Group earned more than $500 million, substantial YoY growth.

The nature of depreciation means that funds from operations, or FFO, is much more important. The company had almost $1.2 billion in FFO, which is what enables the company to drive strong dividends. Versus a $56 billion valuation, that's an almost 9% FFO yield which enables the company to comfortably pay its almost 5% dividend yield.

The company's strong portfolio has enabled it to maintain strong occupancy despite a shift away from malls. The company's at 96% occupancy a 4% increase YoY and the company has also managed to increase its base minimum rent per square foot by 1.3%. Retailer sales per square foot have remained strong which is key to the company continuing rent.

The company increased its dividend by 4.9% YoY, which is impressive given the company's current almost 5% dividend yield.

Simon Property Group Breakdown

Looking at the breakdown, the company's financials remain quite strong and enable it to take advantage of new opportunities.

The company's share price weakness has pushed its valuation to below $70 billion, and the company has minimum preferred equity or any dilution. The company has also not substantially diluted shareholders, which is good to see for an REIT. The company's total revenue has increased YoY and the company's FFO from the operating partnership has enabled the company to grow.

It's impressive to see the company with a mid-single-digit dividend yield and the ability to continue increasing it at a mid-single-digit %.

The company has an incredibly diversified portfolio. Simon Property Group is centered around U.S. malls and premium outlets, and the company is diversified across dozens of states. Almost 20% of the company's revenue comes from Florida, but Florida is a diverse market with a wide range major cities.

The company's diversification means there's minimal geographical risk in our view.

Simon Property Group Financial Breakdown

The company has a diverse portfolio of assets and its rent expirations enable it to have years of stable revenue.

The company has minimal turnover in its anchor leases which are a small % of revenue but a large % of the company's square foot. Still most of these leases go into the 2030s. The company's more valuable renting properties that drive the high rents have valuable leases especially going into the mid-2030s. The company's minimal turnover per year enables it to handle a market downturn.

The company's net debt is sub $30 billion and more than $10 billion in non-recourse mortgage rate. The company's fixed charge coverage is more than enough for the company to handle the current higher interest rates and the company's almost 7 weighted average years to maturity enable it to handle a batch of higher interest rates.

Simon Property Group has done a great job of managing its average interest rate at sub 4% in an expensive market, and with interest rates expected to decline, we don't see any major interest risks for the company. The FFO payout ratio of <70% enables the company to continue paying a strong dividend and investing in its business.

The company has premium properties.

Stanford shopping mall is so busy that users are complaining about how crowded it is. In Pal Alto where it's located, the city's tax revenue has nearly recovered to pre-pandemic levels. That's because the company is benefiting from the rise of third spaces as malls, building elegant modern malls for its businesses.

We expect that to continue to be reflected in the company's future rent recapture.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is another black-swan event. COVID-19 was a black swan event that substantially declined mall traffic and was a major impact and existential threat for Simon Property Group. Another unexpected black swan event could hurt the company's ability to continue its shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Simon Property Group has an impressive portfolio of mall assets as seen with the rise of malls as third spaces. The company has continued to generate substantial FFO, and it's working to increase that FFO. The company offers a mid-single-digit dividend yield and it's continuing to increase that yield by the mid-single-digits.

The company has a diverse portfolio of assets across numerous markets in both the United States and internationally. The company's rent due is manageable and spread across a number of years. Going forward, we expect the company to continue generating strong and incredibly reliable returns, making it a valuable investment.

data.title: GATX Corporation: Still Not Hitching My Future To This Name
data.description:
In May 2024, one company that I decided to rate a "Hold" was GATX Corporation (GATX). The business, which owns a large fleet of rail cars, is certainly the kind of firm that interests me. Having said that, while the overall trend of financial performance for the business was decent, shares were not cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook. Whenever I rate a company a "Hold," I'm making the claim that shares are likely to perform more or less similarly to the broader market for the foreseeable future. And I would say we have had that. Since the time that the article was published, the stock has been up 26.1%. That's a bit above the 23.1% increase seen by the S&P 500 over the same period. But considering how much time has passed, I wouldn't call it a significant disparity.

Looking at the picture today, I see that the firm is cheaper on a forward basis than it was back then. But between how it is priced on an absolute basis and how it is priced compared to other similar enterprises, I'm not ready to upgrade it at this time. This is even after management announced earlier this year a rather large investment that should, in the long run, bode well for shareholders. Given these thoughts, I believe that maintaining the company as a "Hold" candidate is the right choice at this time.

Taking a ride on GATX

Given how much time has elapsed, I think it would be helpful to dig a bit into the overall core operations of GATX. According to management, the company leases, operates, manages, and re-markets certain rail-related assets. It is a pretty large firm. At the end of the most recent quarter, which should be the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, its Rail North America fleet consisted of 102,317 railcars. These boasted a 99.2% utilization rate. That's about as high as you can get.

This does not include boxcars the company does not factor them into these members. Those totaled 7,621 at the end of the most recent quarter. The reason why management does not include these in the other count is simply because they seem to be in the process of gradually phasing out this part of the business. A year earlier, for instance, the company had 8,990 boxcars to its name. But since then, it has scrapped a total of 1,361 while selling off another 35. It added only 27 during this time. For its international operations, which involve its business activities in Europe and India, the business has 30,492 railcars.

There is yet a third operating segment that the company has. But this is quite small in the grand scheme of things. For context, during the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, this segment, known as Engine Leasing, accounted for only 3.4% of the firm's overall revenue, so it barely deserves a mention. The only reason why I discuss it, however, is that it generates a lot of profitability. In the first half of this year, it was responsible for 20.5% of total segment profits. This part of the business consists of certain joint venture ownerships, including a manufacturer of commercial aircraft engines.

Over the last couple of years, we have seen solid growth for the business. In 2024, revenue was $1.59 billion. That's up from the $1.41 billion the company reported the previous year. This was driven by growth across all three of its operating segments.

Rail North America, for instance, saw revenue grow 11.8% from $982.7 million to $1.10 billion. This was thanks to a jump in lease revenue, with management attributing this to a combination of higher lease rates and more railcars on lease. It appears as though most of the change would have had to have been from higher rates. I say this because the number of railcars that were active on an average basis during the year 2024 came in only 1.2% above what the company saw the previous year.

This was not all. For its international operations, the company saw revenue expand from $309.5 million to $350.3 million. An increase in the average active rail car of 1.7% certainly helped. But obviously, higher rates did the heavy lifting. And finally, the Engine Leasing segment reported a rise in revenue from $77.2 million to $97.1 million. This was entirely because non-dedicated engine revenue rose in response to aircraft spare engines acquired during the year.

With revenue rising, profitability for the business also increased. Net income grew from $259.2 million to $284.2 million. We saw operating cash flow jump from $520.4 million to $602.1 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $526.8 million to $603 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $782.6 million to $899.1 million.

For the 2025 fiscal year, we have results for the first two quarters. An increase in average active rail cars and higher rates pushed revenue up for the Rail North America segment to $589 million compared to the $535.4 million the company reported the previous year. That was instrumental in pushing overall revenue for the company up from $766.6 million in the first half of 2024 to $852.1 million at the same time this year.

In its international business, GATX reported a rise in revenue from $168.9 million to $184.3 million. This came as the average active rail car has grown slightly from 28,198 to 28,572. But again, higher lease rates accounted for the lion’s share of the growth. There was also some growth when it came to the Engine Leasing segment, with sales climbing from $43.1 million to $58.2 million. All of this seems to have stemmed from aircraft spare engines acquired last year that were utilized in the engine capacity agreement that the company has with Rolls-Royce.

Net income for the company also jumped, rising from $118.7 million to $154.1 million. We saw operating cash flow expand from $235.9 million to $285.5 million. On an adjusted basis, the increase was from $251.7 million to $292 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $430.2 million to $494.1 million. For the current fiscal year, as a whole, management expects earnings per share of between $8.50 and $8.90. At the midpoint, we would be looking at net profits of $312.3 million compared to the $284.2 million reported for 2024. It's also looking like adjusted operating cash flow will be somewhere around $699.5 million, while EBITDA will be around $1.03 billion.

At the start of this article, I mentioned that management made a rather interesting maneuver earlier this year. Specifically, this was the firm's decision to partner up with institutional firms, namely Brookfield Infrastructure Partners L.P. (BIP), that involve a joint venture formed between the parties that will then subsequently acquire the operating lease portfolio of railcars currently owned by a subsidiary of Wells Fargo & Company (WFC). This is a massive transaction with a value of $4.4 billion.

Initially, ownership interest in this joint venture will be 30% for GATX and 70% for Brookfield. However, GATX will manage the railcars of the joint venture, as well as all railcars and locomotives wholly owned by Brookfield. The details are rather complex, but in short, over time, GATX has the opportunity to acquire up to 100% of the joint venture.

The structure of all of this is rather intriguing. For instance, 70% of the purchase price of the venture will come from committed debt financing of the joint venture which will unfortunately be guaranteed by GATX. The other 30% will be paid for through the two enterprises putting in $1.3 billion in exchange for equity in it.

This deal is not likely to close until early next year. Unfortunately, we don't really have much in the way of further information to gauge how profitable it might be. We do know that third-party estimates peg the growth in annual EBITDA associated with this maneuver as amounting to between 10% and 15% for the business. But because we don't have more concrete figures than this, I think it would be best to value the company excluding the prospect of growth here.

In the chart above, you can see how shares of the company are currently priced using estimates for 2025 and historical results for 2024. On an absolute basis, the stock looks decent but far from great. In the table below, I then compared it to four other similar enterprises. What I found was that, on a price-to-earnings basis, two of the four companies ended up being cheaper. This number increases to three of the four companies on a price-to-operating-cash-flow basis. But when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, our candidate was the most expensive of the group.

Company Price/Earnings Price/Operating Cash Flow EV/EBITDA
GATX Corporation 18.3 8.1 13.4
Trinity Industries, Inc. (TRN) 24.5 5.6 11.7
The Greenbrier Companies, Inc. (GBX) 6.5 4.0 6.1
Wabash National Corporation (WNC) 5.9 4.1 4.4
FreightCar America, Inc. (RAIL) 978.0 8.3 6.3

Takeaway

Based on the data currently available, I believe that GATX is an interesting business. But it's not one that I am particularly enthusiastic about from an investment perspective. Shares are decently priced, but far from cheap. The stock actually looks a bit expensive compared to other similar firms. Yes, management is achieving nice growth, but it's not enough, in my opinion, to justify a bullish outlook at this time. Therefore, I have no problem keeping it rated a "Hold" for now.
